questions.question: What is a Fenwick Tree?

options.A: A compact, binary representation of sparse data structures
options.B: A dynamic data structure supporting both insertion and deletion

questions.explanation: A Fenwick Tree is a bit array used for efficient range sum queries and updates. It's suitable for sparse binary vectors. This structure is also known as a Binary Indexed Tree (BIT).

questions.question: Which of the following is an advantage of using a Fenwick Tree?

options.A: Supports fast updates and range sum queries
options.D: Is suitable for dense data structures

questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees offer a great compromise between query and update time complexity. They can update in O(log n) time and can query a range in O(log n) time, where n is the number of elements.

questions.question: What is a use case for a Fenwick Tree?

options.B: Finding the longest common subsequence between two sequences

questions.explanation: A Fenwick Tree is particularly useful when dealing with sparse data structures and requires efficient range sum queries and updates.

questions.question: Fenwick Trees are often called what?
questions.explanation: Both Fenwick Tree and Binary Indexed Tree (BIT) are used to refer to the same data structure.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a characteristic of a Fenwick Tree?
questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees update in O(log n) time because of the requirement to update the parent node after changing the child node.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of updating a Fenwick Tree?
questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees update in O(log n) time because the size of the parent node is halved with each update.

questions.question: Fenwick Trees are useful when dealing with sparse data structures. What does this mean?

options.A: The data set contains most information in a few areas
options.B: The data set contains most information in almost all areas
options.C: The data set contains no information
options.D: The data set contains all information

questions.explanation: Sparse data structures have the property that most of the elements have a value of 0. Fenwick Trees make use of this property to optimize range sum queries and updates.

questions.question: What data structure is a Fenwick Tree based on?
questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees are based on arrays, specifically bit arrays.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of querying a Fenwick Tree?
questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees query in O(log n) time because it requires traversing through the parent nodes to the root node.

questions.question: When would you prefer to use a Fenwick Tree over an array?

options.A: When dealing with large sparse arrays
options.B: When dealing with large dense arrays
options.D: When dealing with stack data structures

questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees offer efficient range sum queries and updates, which is particularly useful when dealing with sparse data structures.

questions.question: Which of the following operations can be performed on a Fenwick Tree?
questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees primarily support updating, whereas insertion and deletion are not directly supported.

questions.question: Why is a Fenwick Tree suitable for sparse data structures?

options.A: Because it supports fast updates and range sum queries
options.B: Because it has a low storage overhead

questions.explanation: Most of the elements in sparse data structures have a value of 0, and Fenwick Trees are based on bit arrays, which store one bit per element.

questions.question: Which of the following data structures does a Fenwick Tree NOT support?
questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees are specifically designed for dealing with static arrays.

questions.question: How does a Fenwick Tree represent sparse vectors?

options.A: Through the usage of linked nodes
options.B: Through the usage of an array of integers
options.C: Through the usage of bit operations
options.D: Through the usage of hash functions

questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees represent sparse vectors using bit operations, where each bit stands for an element in the vector.

questions.question: Can a Fenwick Tree handle queries that span multiple elements?

options.C: It depends on the elements involved

questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees can efficiently compute range sum queries, which include queries spanning multiple elements.

questions.question: How is a Fenwick Tree typically represented in code?

options.A: Using a binary heap data structure
options.C: Using a linked list data structure

questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees are typically represented as an array of integers, where each integer stores the cumulative sum for the corresponding range.

questions.question: Fenwick Trees can efficiently perform updates, which of the following operations can a Fenwick Tree NOT update in O(1) time?

options.D: Range sum query and individual element sum

questions.explanation: Fenwick Trees can update individual element sums and range sums in O(log n) time. However, updating with bitwise XOR operations cannot be directly supported.
